questions.scenario:
The DevPolaris network team is validating the first production request path.

ALB inbound is TCP 443 from 0.0.0.0/0. Task inbound is TCP 3000 from sg-orders-alb. DB inbound is TCP 5432 from sg-admin-bastion.

Checkout returns 500 and app logs later show database timeouts. Which rule is missing from the intended path?

options.text: Task inbound TCP 3000 from `0.0.0.0/0`.
DB inbound TCP 5432 from `sg-orders-task`.
ALB inbound TCP 5432 from `sg-orders-db`.
DB inbound TCP 443 from the public ALB security group.

questions.explanation:
The intended database hop is from the orders API tasks to RDS on port `5432`. Admin access does not help the app tier connect. Opening the task or database more broadly would hide the real missing hop and weaken the design.

Learn more in [VPC security groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-security-groups.html).

questions.scenario:
A replacement orders task initially cannot read its database secret. After the IAM policy is corrected, startup advances but readiness still fails. The incident timeline is:

```text
09:20 task started
09:21 Secrets Manager returned AccessDeniedException
09:24 IAM policy updated
09:29 secret read succeeded
09:30 PostgreSQL rejected password for user orders_app
```

Where should the team investigate next?

options.text: Inspect the database security group first because a timeout can indicate that the task cannot establish TCP 5432.
Rotate the database user's password immediately because authentication failure proves the stored secret is stale.
The secret value and database user, because the TCP path reached database authentication.
Rotate the database password first because the earlier Secrets Manager denial may have returned a stale credential.

questions.explanation:
The symptom moved again. A password authentication failure means the app reached the database server far enough to attempt login. That is different from IAM denial and different from a TCP timeout.

Learn more in [VPC security groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-security-groups.html).

questions.scenario:
CloudWatch adds this line after the target health check:

```text
path=/health
status=500
reason=db_not_ready
cause=database connection timeout
db_port=5432
```

The DB security group currently allows:

TCP 5432 from sg-admin-bastion

Which fix direction matches the evidence?

options.text: Point `orders.devpolaris.com` to the RDS endpoint so the browser bypasses the ALB.
Change the ALB listener from HTTPS 443 to database port 5432.
Allow TCP 5432 from `sg-orders-api` or `sg-orders-task` on the database security group.
Delete the health check so unhealthy tasks can still receive customer traffic.

questions.explanation:
The app reached its health handler, then failed on the private database hop. The database allows only the admin group today. The fix is to add the intended app task group as a source while keeping customer and ALB traffic out of the database path.

Learn more in [VPC security groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-security-groups.html).

questions.scenario:
The DevPolaris network team is reviewing a first production deployment across a VPC, public entry point, private application tasks, and database. Which statements correctly interpret the proposed boundaries?

VPC CIDR is 10.20.0.0/16. Public-a is 10.20.0.0/24 in us-east-1a. App-a is 10.20.10.0/24 in us-east-1a. Db-a is 10.20.20.0/24 in us-east-1a. Public-b is 10.20.1.0/24 in us-east-1b. App-b is 10.20.11.0/24 in us-east-1b. Db-b is 10.20.21.0/24 in us-east-1b.

options.text: Each subnet belongs to exactly one Availability Zone.
The database subnets become public because their CIDR blocks are inside the VPC CIDR.
The VPC gives all listed subnets one shared private address range.
The design separates public entry, private app, and private database placement.
Use one route table for public and private subnets, relying on security groups to prevent private resources from using the internet gateway.

questions.explanation:
The VPC CIDR is the larger private range, and each subnet is an AZ-specific slice of it. The names show a layered placement plan, but public behavior still depends on route tables. Being inside the VPC CIDR does not make a database subnet public.

Learn more in [VPC subnet basics](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/configure-subnets.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris is designing the first production VPC for the orders service. A teammate proposes placing the internet-facing ALB, ECS tasks, and RDS database in `subnet-app-a` because the subnet is already inside `vpc-orders-prod`. That subnet belongs only to Availability Zone `us-east-1a`, while the service requirement is to continue serving checkout if one Availability Zone fails. What is the most important concern with the proposal?

options.text: Separate ALB, tasks, and RDS into public and private subnets but keep all subnets in the same Availability Zone.
A subnet lives in one Availability Zone, so putting everything in one subnet also puts everything in one AZ.
Place the ALB and tasks in public subnets but leave RDS private, using security groups as the main exposure boundary.
Keep every tier private and publish the service through a NAT gateway so inbound traffic can reach the ALB.

questions.explanation:
Subnets are smaller slices of a VPC, and each subnet belongs to one Availability Zone. Spreading app and database placement across AZ-specific subnets improves the shape of the design. The VPC can contain many subnets.

Learn more in [VPC subnet basics](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/configure-subnets.html).

questions.scenario:
The private app subnet route table says:

```text
Destination      Target
10.20.0.0/16     local
0.0.0.0/0        nat-0aaa2222
```

The app task needs to connect to RDS at `10.20.21.18:5432`. Which route handles that packet?

options.text: Use the local route for traffic to another subnet in the same VPC, and use NAT only for destinations outside the VPC.
Use the NAT route for all traffic from a private subnet, including traffic to RDS in another private subnet.
Use the most specific matching route, so an endpoint prefix route can take precedence over the default NAT route.
`10.20.0.0/16 -> local`, because the database address is inside the VPC CIDR.

questions.explanation:
AWS uses the most specific matching destination. `10.20.21.18` fits inside `10.20.0.0/16`, so the local route keeps the packet inside the VPC. NAT is for destinations outside the VPC; this database hop stays local.

Learn more in [VPC route tables](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/VPC_Route_Tables.html).

questions.scenario:
Users see `502 Bad Gateway`. The ALB subnet check returns:

```text
subnet-0puba  us-east-1a
subnet-0pubb  us-east-1b
```

Target health returns:

```text
10.20.10.45  us-east-1a  healthy
10.20.11.88  us-east-1b  unhealthy  Target.Timeout
```

What question should drive the next check?

options.text: What is different about the app subnet, route table, or access path for the unhealthy target in us-east-1b?
Why is the public ALB using two subnets when one healthy target exists?
Can the RDS database be moved into the public ALB subnets to make both targets healthy?
Should Route 53 be changed because target health includes private IP addresses?

questions.explanation:
The ALB is enabled in both expected AZs, and one target is healthy. The unhealthy target is specific to one AZ, so compare that target's subnet, route table, and security path. Do not jump to DNS or database placement before checking the differing hop.

Learn more in [Application Load Balancer target health](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/target-group-health-checks.html).
